current_fact.fact_text: The London Naval Treaty, signed on April 22, 1930, tried to cap a global arms race by limiting the size and number of warships and regulating submarine warfare. Ironically, the treaty’s technical tonnage formulas and clauses turned battleships into creatures of international bureaucracy as much as of steel.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: A treaty about controlling fleets and global routes fits with this episode’s theme of navigating ‘new territories’—only here the voyage is through sound instead of warships.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1898, as the Spanish–American War loomed, U.S. President William McKinley called for 125,000 volunteers and Congress rushed to more than double the size of the regular army. In a matter of days, a primarily agrarian society began transforming itself into a modern military power with overseas ambitions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: The Spanish–American War signaled a new era of U.S. power projection; pairing it with a fiercely anti-imperialist, political rock episode highlights the tensions behind such mobilizations.

queue.fact_text: During World War I’s Second Battle of Ypres, on April 22, 1915, German forces released over 150 tons of chlorine gas into the wind, unleashing one of the first large-scale chemical weapon attacks in history. The greenish-yellow cloud caused panic and horrific casualties, and forced soldiers to improvise gas masks from urine-soaked cloths.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: This dark turning point in warfare suits a moody, cinematic trip-hop journey; the episode’s shadowy textures echo the eerie, drifting gas clouds that changed battlefields forever.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1944, in remote Greenland, a tiny Allied Sledge Patrol attacked and destroyed the German Bassgeiger weather station, part of a secret ‘weather war’ in the North Atlantic. In icy isolation, the battle wasn’t over territory or treasure but over meteorological data that could decide the fate of convoys and fleets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden strike on a lonely Arctic station feels like a literal ‘electric shock’ in the frozen quiet; the episode’s high-voltage guitars mirror the abrupt violence in an otherwise still landscape.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 22, 1944, Allied forces began Operation Persecution, landing near Hollandia in New Guinea to seize Japanese airfields. The assault used surprise amphibious landings to leapfrog enemy strongholds, turning remote tropical shores into improvised runways and supply hubs.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A complex, multi-stage amphibious operation suits a ‘crescendo’ mix that slowly builds and then spreads across regions, echoing how the landings unfolded into a wider campaign.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1944, the U.S. 1st Air Commando Group used Sikorsky R-4 helicopters in Burma, carrying out the first combat search-and-rescue missions in history. These fragile two-seat machines, with exposed frameworks and limited power, nevertheless proved that rotorcraft could pluck wounded soldiers from places no airplane could safely reach.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: These pioneering rescues were about dropping into difficult terrain and lifting people back out; the episode’s theme of immersion and fluid motion parallels the helicopter’s new ability to move vertically through space.

queue.fact_text: The Battle of Eckmühl reached its decisive second day on April 22, 1809, when Napoleon’s forces outmaneuvered and defeated the Austrian army near Regensburg. Using rapid marches and concentrated artillery, he pushed the Austrians back over the Danube, turning a scattered campaign into a sudden French triumph.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s aggressive push at Eckmühl mirrors the unapologetic drive of women in rock who refuse to ask for permission—the same energy, channeled into guitars instead of cannon.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1951, Chinese forces launched a major assault on UN positions held by Australian and Canadian units at the Battle of Kapyong during the Korean War. Against overwhelming numbers, the defenders held key ground in brutal night fighting, helping prevent the fall of Seoul.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: Kapyong’s intense, almost ritualized night combat resonates with Ghost’s theatrical, warlike rock liturgy—both are about holding a line under overwhelming pressure.

queue.fact_text: Beginning April 22, 1954, the Army–McCarthy hearings were broadcast live on American television, bringing the drama of Senator Joseph McCarthy’s anti-communist accusations into living rooms. For many viewers, it was the first time they could watch political power being challenged in real time, helping to turn public opinion against McCarthy’s methods.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Those televised hearings were an early moment of media reshaping politics; this episode’s reflection on screens, connection, and overload updates that story for the smartphone age.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1954, the Army–McCarthy hearings also marked a milestone for television as a medium of mass persuasion. The now-famous rebuke—“Have you no sense of decency, sir, at long last?”—was captured on camera, becoming one of the first viral moments of broadcast-era political theater.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Because the hearings showed how powerful mediated attention could be, pairing them with an episode about how we focus and consume media today highlights how our relationship with screens keeps evolving.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1969, British sailor Robin Knox-Johnston completed the first solo non-stop circumnavigation of the globe, winning the Sunday Times Golden Globe Race. For 312 days he faced storms, loneliness, and equipment failures alone, becoming the only contestant to finish the perilous challenge.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A record-breaking solo voyage by sail could only match with an episode literally recorded on a boat—turning Knox-Johnston’s solitary odyssey into a communal, musical sea journey.

queue.fact_text: The Oklahoma Land Rush of April 22, 1889, saw tens of thousands of settlers line up along a border and then charge in at noon to claim plots of former Indigenous land. In a matter of hours, tent cities like Oklahoma City and Guthrie appeared, each with populations of at least 10,000 people.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: The frantic start of the land rush—everyone racing forward at once—fits perfectly with an ‘on the go’ soundtrack about sudden movement, new beginnings, and kinetic energy.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1977, optical fiber was used for the first time to carry live telephone traffic, turning beams of light into clear conversations. This quiet technical milestone paved the way for today’s high-speed internet, where undersea fiber cables carry most of the world’s digital whispers and shouts.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: Fiber optics quietly launched a new era of continuous, flowing digital communication; this introductory ‘Evening Flow’ episode mirrors that idea of seamless, rule-free transmission through sound.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2005, Japanese Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi issued a formal apology for Japan’s wartime aggression, acknowledging the ‘tremendous damage and suffering’ inflicted on neighboring countries. The speech was part of a long, contested process of historical reckoning and regional reconciliation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: An official apology is a kind of political clearing of the skies after long storms; this episode’s theme—‘It Can’t Rain Forever’—captures the fragile hope of trying to move toward a more honest, lighter future.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1970, Chicano activists in San Diego occupied land beneath the Coronado Bridge, refusing to leave until it was turned into a community park. Their sit-in led to the creation of Chicano Park, now famous for its massive murals that transform highway pillars into vivid, political artwork.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Chicano Park’s murals and activism grow from Mexican-American and broader Hispanic culture; a deep dive into flamenco roots and Spanish expression connects to that same Iberian-Latin artistic lineage.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1944, the experimental Sikorsky R-4 helicopter proved so transformative that many pilots began imagining a future where vertical flight was as common as cars. The spindly two-seat craft was underpowered and awkward, but it quietly opened the door to air ambulances, rooftop landings, and the idea of cities seen from hovering above.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

queue.match_reason: Helicopters changed how we occupy vertical space; this ‘Space Shift’ episode invites a similar change in perspective—seeing open air and new dimensions as creative possibilities.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2025, terrorists attacked a group of tourists in Pahalgam, Jammu and Kashmir, killing at least 26 people in a mountain town better known for its natural beauty and pilgrim trails. The attack was claimed by The Resistance Front, linked to the militant group Lashkar-e-Taiba, underscoring how even remote, scenic places can become sites of global violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A brutal act of political violence in a beautiful place calls for an artist who faces violence and power head-on; Residente’s raw, critical storytelling offers space to process anger, grief, and resistance.

queue.fact_text: Across all these April 22 events, one thread is our planet itself: from Earth Day marches and climate treaties to wars fought for territory, land rushes, and parks reclaimed for murals and community. This single date on the calendar holds a cross-section of how humans shape, divide, damage, and try to heal the Earth they stand on.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: Afrobeat, rooted in political critique and planetary groove, offers a fitting closing pulse for a day of Earth-shaped stories—turning reflection on land and power into rhythm and collective movement.

published.fact_text: On April 22, 1970, the very first Earth Day was celebrated in the United States, mobilizing an estimated 20 million people to march, teach, and protest for environmental protection. This grassroots wave of action helped push the U.S. government to create the Environmental Protection Agency and pass landmark clean air and water laws.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Earth Day’s call to slow down and protect the planet pairs with this reflective, ‘slow planet’ soundscape about gentler rhythms and environmental calm.

published.fact_text: The 2016 signing of the Paris Agreement marked the first time nearly every nation on Earth formally committed to tackling climate change together. Countries agreed to limit global warming to well below 2 °C, with an ambitious aim of 1.5 °C, making this treaty a planetary pact about our shared future climate.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The Paris Agreement is humanity learning—slowly—how to change; this episode’s focus on long-term learning and persistence mirrors the treaty’s difficult, incremental climate journey.

published.fact_text: When the first Earth Day was held on April 22, 1970, students turned campuses into makeshift laboratories and town squares into open-air classrooms. Many of the day’s events blended science, music, and protest, turning environmental concern into a kind of cultural festival for the planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: As Earth Day raised awareness of fragile ecosystems, this episode dives into one of the most charismatic and vulnerable ones—Arctic seas and whales—turning environmental concern into intimate storytelling.

published.fact_text: On April 22, 1500, Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral’s fleet sighted and landed on the coast of what is now Brazil, claiming it for Portugal. What was officially framed as a ‘discovery’ actually connected Europe with lands long inhabited by Indigenous peoples with rich, complex cultures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: Cabral’s transoceanic voyage echoes this episode’s imagery of boats and seafaring routes, but here the journey is recast through coastal music rather than colonial conquest.

published.fact_text: The Treaty of Zaragoza, signed on April 22, 1529, drew an imaginary line across the eastern hemisphere to divide newly ‘discoverable’ lands between Spain and Portugal. With a few strokes of ink, distant monarchs attempted to partition oceans and islands they had never seen, including the spice-rich Moluccas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: Where the treaty carved up the world from faraway palaces, this episode crosses borders through personal stories and songs, gently subverting the idea that land and seas can be cleanly divided.

published.fact_text: On April 22, 1519, Hernán Cortés founded a settlement at Veracruz on the Gulf of Mexico, creating his beachhead for the eventual conquest of the Aztec Empire. The small coastal camp became a pivot point where European ambitions collided with Mesoamerican civilizations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Cortés’s landing foreshadows centuries of cultural collision in Latin America; this episode picks up that history’s musical aftershocks through reggaeton’s hybrid, post-colonial sound.

published.fact_text: The London Naval Treaty, signed on April 22, 1930, tried to cap a global arms race by limiting the size and number of warships and regulating submarine warfare. Ironically, the treaty’s technical tonnage formulas and clauses turned battleships into creatures of international bureaucracy as much as of steel.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A treaty about controlling fleets and global routes fits with this episode’s theme of navigating ‘new territories’—only here the voyage is through sound instead of warships.
